issues.original: The first eleven articles in this series tackled **continuous convex** problems (or convex relaxations of non-convex ones). This final article confronts two harder regimes:
issues.fixed: The first eleven articles in this series tackled **continuous convex** problems (or convex relaxations of non-convex ones). This final article addresses two harder regimes:

issues.original: Linear and convex tools no longer apply directly — there are no derivatives across the integer lattice.
issues.fixed: Linear and convex tools no longer apply directly because there are no derivatives across the integer lattice.

issues.original: Both regimes share a key feature: **provably optimal algorithms are exponential** in the worst case. Practical solutions come from (a) exact algorithms with smart pruning (branch-and-bound), and (b) heuristics that find good (not optimal) solutions in polynomial time.
issues.fixed: Both regimes share a key feature: **provably optimal algorithms are exponential** in the worst case. Practical solutions come from (a) exact algorithms with smart pruning (branch-and-bound) and (b) heuristics that find good (but not optimal) solutions in polynomial time.

issues.original: This article: 1. Reviews integer programming and the branch-and-bound algorithm with the LP relaxation as the bounding mechanism. 2. Surveys the heuristic landscape — particle swarm, genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, spiral optimization — and where each fits. 3. Walks through a substantial case study: applying the Spiral Optimization Algorithm to a constrained mean-variance portfolio problem (a mixed-integer nonlinear program, MINLP).
issues.fixed: This article: 1. Reviews integer programming and the branch-and-bound algorithm, using the LP relaxation as the bounding mechanism. 2. Surveys the heuristic landscape, including particle swarm, genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, and spiral optimization, and explains where each fits. 3. Walks through a substantial case study: applying the Spiral Optimization Algorithm to a constrained mean-variance portfolio problem (a mixed-integer nonlinear program, MINLP).

issues.original: The naive approach — enumerate all [FORMULA] values of [FORMULA], solve an LP for each — is hopeless for [FORMULA]. Branch-and-bound is what makes practical MILPs solvable.
issues.fixed: The naive approach — enumerating all [FORMULA] values of [FORMULA] and solving an LP for each — is impractical for [FORMULA]. Branch-and-bound makes practical MILPs solvable.

issues.original: If the LP relaxation happens to have integer-valued [FORMULA], we are done. Otherwise, pick a fractional [FORMULA] and **branch**.
issues.fixed: If the LP relaxation has integer-valued [FORMULA], we are done. Otherwise, pick a fractional [FORMULA] and **branch**.

issues.original: In the best case, branch-and-bound prunes most of the tree and finds the optimum after solving polynomially many LPs. In the worst case it still enumerates all [FORMULA] leaves — confirming that the algorithm is exponential in the worst case.
issues.fixed: In the best case, branch-and-bound prunes most of the tree and finds the optimum after solving a polynomial number of LPs. In the worst case, it still enumerates all [FORMULA] leaves, confirming that the algorithm is exponential in the worst case.

issues.original: A **cutting plane** is an inequality [FORMULA] that is satisfied by every integer-feasible [FORMULA] but violated by the current LP relaxation optimum. Adding cuts tightens the LP relaxation, raising the lower bound and pruning more aggressively.
issues.fixed: A **cutting plane** is an inequality [FORMULA] that is satisfied by every integer-feasible [FORMULA] but violated by the current LP relaxation optimum. Adding cuts tightens the LP relaxation, raising the lower bound and allowing more aggressive pruning.
